abstract: Social media today are playing a more important role as a news source than ever before. Yet, there have been no longitudinal studies on journalists' sourcing practices in recent years that allow us to consider the mechanisms of innovation diffusion. Comparative studies of different social platforms in different media systems are just as rare. We therefore examine the use of Facebook and Twitter as journalistic sources in newspapers of three countries. A main finding is that, after a period of stagnation at the beginning of this decade, the use of social media sources has resurged massively in recent years. The patterns of this second rise of social media in journalism are almost identical in the analyzed newspapers. A comparison of the platforms has shown that Twitter is more commonly used as a news source than Facebook. Compared to Facebook, Twitter is primarily used as an elite channel. An unsupervised topic clustering approach (LDA) also revealed that the issues on which social media are sourced and the quantities of social media references are similar in The New York Times and The Guardian. In S€ uddeutsche Zeitung, however, journalists source social media considerably less, and in different thematic contexts.

paragraphs: Social media are an innovation for journalism on many levels. They enable journalists to spread and advertise content via new channels and to facilitate interactive contact with the reader. As a source, they also promise faster access to elites, to the voice of the people, and to regions of the world that are otherwise difficult to access. In a recent survey among US journalists, only 9% of respondents stated that social media are not an important source to them (Cision 2017, 9). "Like" and "Share" metrics have become news factors, turning the space of discourse into a "beat" by which reporters find new ways to "gather and share and negotiate information with sources. By doing so, they are assimilated into the social network that constitutes a beat" (Broersma and Graham 2013, 447). This assimilation harbors opportunity as well as risk. On one hand, it offers a chance to break through traditional routines, open new spaces for discourse, and elevate the deliberative potential of the platforms. On the other hand, it holds the risk of dependence on global corporations whose opaque algorithms are building an everstronger monopoly over how social communication is organized.

Recent studies on the correlation between journalism and social media mostly focused on the propagation of journalistic content via social media. They pointed out that the platforms' policies increasingly force journalism into a critical dependence on social media. As Caplan and Boyd point out, "long-standing news outlets must construct their content with algorithmic and data-centric intermediaries in mind" (2018, 1). Nielsen and Ganter warn that news media "are becoming dependent upon increasingly powerful digital intermediaries" (2018,1602). The social media corporations' stranglehold on journalistic partners and their content makes the platforms a vital public sphere, and, as such, an important source of journalistic research and for constant monitoring of public debatesdependency on social media is growing not only on the distribution, but also on the production side. This is a particularly critical aspect since social media are increasingly being used for (partially automated) distribution of targeted misinformation, which journalists often struggle to disprove. In a recent survey among journalists, 48% of respondents indicated that they could not do their jobs without social media, while 51% state that "fake news is a serious problem" to journalism (Cision 2017, 2).

Given these developments, our research question of how journalists source social media is highly topical. It seems all the more urgent since the social media platforms' strategies of convergence have intensified in the past few years (Bell 2016). In their literature review on "Social Media, Political Polarization, and Political Disinformation," Tucker et al. (2018, 60) point out that links between social media and traditional media represent one of the substantive gaps in this research field. The fact that there are no recent content analyses on social media as a journalistic source emphasizes the need for a current study (see Table 1).

In view of the above, the objective of this paper is to update early evidence of newspaper journalists' social media sourcing. We examine sourcing techniques over a longer period, in particular to observe any possible shift in practices during the phases observed: from the moment these platforms first emerged until late 2016. In addition, an international comparison of newspapers of three western countries (US, UK, and Germany) will show how the relationship between journalism and social media might vary depending on national circumstances. Also, we will compare Twitter and Facebook sources to verify whether our findings apply to social media in general or whether journalists use different platforms in different ways.

paragraphs: The process described in this article, which needs a theoretical classification, is the process of journalists adapting the innovative tools of Twitter and Facebook. In this context, both media can be considered important representatives of the innovation cluster "social media." 1 Rogers (2003) describes diffusion as a "process in which an innovation is communicated through certain channels over time among the members of a social system" (5). Early diffusion studies in communication science explored the diffusion of news (Deutschmann and Danielson, 1960;Rogers, 2003) and later, from the 1970, the diffusion of various technologies (e.g., cable television, Krugman 1985, audiotext, Lin andAtkin 2002). Other studies have also harnessed innovation diffusion theory to analyze the adaption of information technologies in newspaper newsrooms (Garrison 2001), thus taking an organization-level perspective, which we will take as well.

The diffusion process is divided into the phases of awareness (knowledge), interest, persuasion, decision/adoption, and confirmation. Process analyses usually explore the attributes of innovation, in particular their advantages and compatibility, the innovation rate, the network through which diffusion is driven, especially its change agents and opinion leaders, as well as the consequences of such innovation.

Newer approaches in diffusion innovation theory, which have adapted the original theory for the adaption of new technologies into the system of journalistic organizations, provide a suitable framework for this study. Ekdale et al. (2015) differentiated the concept of stages of implementation with a special focus on social media. They distinguish between three interconnected waves of changes: changes in technology use, audience relationships, and professional culture. The former faces the least amount of obstacles, since "journalists are increasingly convinced of the meritsindeed, the necessityof adapting their practices to newer technological capabilities" (Ekdale et al. 2015, 939). Ekdale et al. stress that journalists are open to changes that enhance the quality of their work and are consistent with their norms and values, while they resist changes that restrict their autonomy and damage the journalistic product (939). Journalists are thus considered conservative with regard to technological change (Reich 2013)accordingly, the first wave is marked by an adaption of new tools to fit existing practices, not to transform them.

The second wave, however, brings a reformulation of the relationship between journalists and their communities (Ekdale et al. 2015, 942). Members of the public are now also critics and partners in content creation. While these new opportunities for interaction brought about new forms of collaboration and strategies (Hermida 2012;Holten and Lewis 2011), another repercussion is a constant tension between the network ethic of participation and a sense of loss of control on the part of the journalists (Lewis 2012).

The third wave involves a reconfiguration of professional culture as news organizations experiment with new ways of doing business (Ekdale et al. 2015, 942). This wave of innovation is mostly about structural changes within a newsroom, for example, strategies of newsroom convergence, which play a minor role in this study.

In particular, tensions between the first and second wave are also reflected in research on adaption of social media as a journalistic source: Journalists tend to be skeptical about gleaning the "voice of the people" from social media (Bosch 2014, 36;Gulyas 2013, 281). The normalization hypothesis (Lasorsa, Lewis, and Holton 2012;Singer 2005) thus states that journalists are sticking "to traditional gatekeeping practices and shying away from sharing the stage with the public" (Parmelee et al. 2017, 11). Accordingly, earlier studies concluded that journalists maintain their conventional routines (Moon and Hadley 2014, 289;Paulussen and Harder 2014, 3). Overall, studies concur that social media reproduce the traditional "hierarchy of credibility" (Becker 1967;Hermida, Lewis, and Zamith 2014;Reich 2011) and do not "diminish the power of elite sources" ( Paulussen and Harder 2014, 8; cf. also Wallsten 2015, Skogerbø et al. 2016).
Tying into innovation of diffusion theory and its further development by Ekdale et al., and building on the current state of research, this content-analytical study will explore three main elements:

1. Changes in technology use: Rogers (2003) divides the process of adaption into two general phases: initiation and implementation. The latter involves redefining and restructuring, that is, the adaptation of the innovation to specific needs and structures within the organization; clarifying, which is the phase when more and more individuals begin to appreciate that implementing the innovation makes sense and the innovation thus begins to spread; and routinizing, when the innovation is incorporated into the organization's regular routines. Based on the state of research, it is difficult to assess which stage of the diffusion cycle social media has currently reached in the social system of journalistic organizations. Previous content analyses of social media sourcing only cover the early diffusion phase (see Table 1). Results of other studies indicate that the decisive phase of adaption occurred around the year 2010 (Newman 2011, 14); some ascertained a stagnation a few years later: For instance, regarding the diffusion of social media use by Swedish journalists, Djerf-Pierre et al. ( 2016) ascertained that the innovation curve did not flatten until 2014. "At present, we have reached the point where the journalists who are still in the process of appropriating social media belong to the group of late laggards" (858). The social media hype in journalism, they conclude, might be coming to an end, as indicated by the fact that the perceived usefulness of social media dropped dramatically in 2014 compared to 2012. The Cision study, conducted annually among journalists, concluded that the period between 2013 and 2017 saw no considerable shifts in journalists' professional social media use. This is "interesting given the growth in social media brands and changes in the media industries during the period" (Cision 2017, 6). The authors conclude that social media are now fully embedded as technological infrastructures in journalistic work routines.

Studies on the formation of new professional profiles, such as engagement and social media editors (Ferrer-Conill and Tandoc 2018), might also suggest that newsrooms are already caught up in the third wave of change, as described by Ekdale et al., the change in professional culture. The question that remains to be answered is whether the adoption of social media as a news source is still in its implementation stage, or whether it has already reached a later stage such as stagnation or even regression. 2. Changes in audience relationships: The dangers of a normalization of social media have been pointed out by various commentators. Reporters retreat into an echo chamber with fellow journalists and political insiders (Parmelee 2014, 22;Lee et al. 2016, 849). Also, politicians and other social actors increasingly harness journalists' affinity for social media sourcing to circumvent "negotiation-through-conversation" (Broersma and Graham 2013, 449), as was evident in the 2016 US presidential election campaign (Bruns 2018, 198). Accordingly, there are calls to "design your way out of the filter bubble" (Bradshaw 2016: para. 25), and some more recent interviews indicate that SOURCING THE SOURCES journalists are trying to use social media in a more diverse manner (Parmelee 2014, 443). We analyze the changes in the audience relationship by examining whom journalists cite when they use social media sources: elite sources, as the normalization hypothesis would suggest, or nonelite sources.

Accordingly, there are calls to "design your way out of the filter bubble" (Bradshaw 2016: para. 25), and some more recent interviews indicate that SOURCING THE SOURCES journalists are trying to use social media in a more diverse manner (Parmelee 2014, 443). We analyze the changes in the audience relationship by examining whom journalists cite when they use social media sources: elite sources, as the normalization hypothesis would suggest, or nonelite sources.

sections.paper_id: Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: The attributes help understand why some ideas diffuse faster and some lag behind. On the one hand, attributes are operationalized by the category of relative advantages as a "degree to which an innovation is perceived as being better than the idea it supersedes" (Rogers 2003, 257). A comparison of a cluster consisting of Twitter and Facebook highlights the relative advantages of one social medium over another. Since we focus on their adaption as a journalistic source, the perceived advantages of each social medium are identified by the topics in whose context they are sourced. For instance, if Twitter were cited as a source in the context of disasters, it could have a perceived advantage regarding that specific topicin particular if the same were not true for Facebook.

So far, we have mostly generalizing and no comparative statements about the thematic context of social media sourcing. Studies found a clear tendency toward soft news (Broersma and Graham 2013, 454;Moon and Hadley 2014, 300;Paulussen and Harder 2014, 6) and international coverage (Paulussen and Harder 2014, 6). The analysis by Van Leuven, Heinrich, and Deprez on sourcing during the Arab Spring showed that journalists source social media more frequently in times of "information chaos" (2015,586). As other studies demonstrate, this is also true for other situations in which information from official sources is initially scarce, like in the aftermath of terror attacks (Williams, Wardle, and Wahl-Jorgensen 2011) or natural disasters (Bruno 2011).

In addition, the relative quantity of use will be indicative for the innovation attribute of compatibility - Rogers (2003) defines it as a "degree to which an innovation is perceived as consistent with existing values, past experiences and needs of potential adopters" (258). Twitter's structure as an alternative news service, in particular, was described as highly compatible with journalistic practice in breaking news situations (Hermida 2010). Accordingly, Parmelee (2014) ascertained that political journalists use Twitter far more heavily than Facebook (23).

sections.paper_id: Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: Using a methodically uniform long-term study covering the period since the launch of the platforms Twitter (2006) and Facebook (2004) until the end of 2016, we assess whether journalists' use of sources has changedor whether there has been a normalization. We pose three explorative research questions to examine the changes in technology use (RQ1), the changes in audience relationships (RQ2) and the innovation attributes (RQ3) of the two social media platforms (Twitter, Facebook): RQ1: Has the usage curve of social media sourcing reached the stagnation or even the regression phase? RQ2: Has the ratio of elite/nonelite sources changed during the diffusion phase of the innovation?
RQ3: Which innovation attributes can be identified by a quantitative comparison of social media and the respective thematic context of social media sourcing?

sections.paper_id: Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: Several studies recommend a comparative approach when analyzing sourcing practices in order to protect researchers from making inadmissible generalizations: For instance, in their comparative study of Czech journalists' use of Facebook versus Twitter, Hlad ık and St etka (2017) found indications "that caution against generalizations about the role of social media in journalism and signal the requirement for a more nuanced approach to individual platforms" (154). In her international comparison between the UK, the USA, and Germany, Gulyas found "significant differences between surveyed countries, suggesting that cultural factors are important in how relationship with the audience is developing" (2017,401). Therefore, this study will examine the sourcing practices of three metropolitan, liberal national newspapers (The New York Times, The Guardian, and S€ uddeutsche Zeitung) located in Western countries (USA, UK, and Germany) regarding two social media channels (Facebook, Twitter). We cannot be certain that the selected newspapers are representative of their respective journalistic culture with regard to the studied criterion. However, based on their status as leading media, their high circulation and their dominating presence in social media, we can assume that considerable differences between them will at least be indicative of general cultural differences.

Regarding the general distribution of social media and their use as a news source, the following similarities apply across countries: According to Reuters Digital News Report (Newman et al. 2017), use of Twitter in the UK and the USA is similar (12/ 15% for news, 25/26% total) whereas in Germany, it is comparatively low (4% for news, 11% total). Use of Facebook for news is strikingly high in the USA (48%, total: 71%) and similar in the two other countries (UK: 29%, total: 65%, Germany: 25%, total: 51%). This might help explain journalistic activity, because other studies also found that, for instance, "countries with large potential Twitter audiences also host news outlets with large and active actual Twitter audiences" (Engesser and Humprecht 2015, 523). Additionally, current findings plausibly suggest that a general affinity with social media could go hand in hand with increased use of social media sources. Gulyas found that journalists from countries with "large potential Twitter audiences use Twitter more frequently" (2017,525).

Comparisons between media of northern European and Atlantic countries are likely to yield differences simply because of their disparate media systems (Hallin and Mancini 2004). Thus, in a similar comparison (northern European: NL, Atlantic: UK), Broersma and Graham come to the conclusion that British newspapers use Twitter as a source more frequently due to their more competitive newspaper market. The bleaker economic situation of British media makes Twitter an appealing way to rationalize research efforts (2013,452). At the same time, the tabloidized British newspaper market trends toward soft news, even when using Twitter sources: only 6% of tweets cited stem from politicians (NL: over 20%); by contrast, 36% from celebrities (NL: 12%).

In her study, Gulyas also found that the UK stands out due to its "competitively oriented journalistic culture" (2017, 277). English journalists voiced the most positive SOURCING THE SOURCES attitudes toward social media -Broersma und Graham refer to The Guardian as "a frontrunner in adopting Twitter" (2013,451). German journalists displayed the most negative attitudes (Gulyas 2017, 280).

At the same time, the speed of adaptation and thus the current diffusion phase in each country might vary. A country whose journalists adapted social media very early on might already be in the phase of disillusionment. Rather like the conclusions drawn by (Djerf-Pierre, Ghersetti, and Hedman 2016) regarding Sweden, Engesser and Humprecht find that the USA may already have reached the end of the diffusion processwhile Western Europe "may still be in the phase of enthusiasm" (2015,526).

sections.paper_id: Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: For each newspaper, we generated a corpus using the search terms "facebook" and "twitter/tweet" (each as patterns). The resulting articles can be separated in three categories: Firstly, texts referring to the newspapers' own social media activities, but not focusing on social media in any other way, and not using social media as a source (error category 1). Secondly, texts referencing social media platforms, but not as a source for the text, but rather, for example, focusing on Facebook as a company, "Generation Twitter," or general user statistics (error category 2). Thirdly, texts in which journalists use the search terms to indicate the origin of the information (source-articles).

The latter category is the one that is relevant for this study. Thus, for our analysis, texts in this category had to be separated from texts of error categories 1 and 2. We did this following these three steps 2 :

1. We generated subcorpora C1 from the full corpora of SZ, NYT, and The Guardian in the period between 2004 and 2016 (N total NYT ¼ 117,5636/ N total Guardian ¼ 129,8334/N total SZ ¼ 85,1667) using the search terms "facebook" (N c1 NYT FB ¼33,980/N c1 Guardian FB ¼25,420/N c1 SZ FB ¼8,851) and "twitter/tweet" (N c1 NYT TW ¼40,575/N c1 Guardian TW ¼59,312/N c1 SZ TW ¼7,356). 2. We deleted error category 1 sentences such as "Follow The New York Times on Twitter" from the corpus using regular expressions (see Supplementary material for the complete list). We performed such regular expression operations for each dataset C1. The repeated reduction in the corpora to texts containing the search terms results in corpora C2 for (N c2 NYT FB ¼21,102/ N c2 Guardian FB ¼20,065/N c2 SZ FB ¼8,598) and "twitter/tweet" (N c2 NYT TW ¼27,316/ N c2 Guardian TW ¼38,606/N c2 SZ TW ¼6,784). 3. We drew a representative sample from each of the six corpora C2 containing six sets of 900 articles (N ¼ 5,400). Three coders labeled the samples in two coding categories. As the first coding category, coders had to decide if the article was from error category 2 or a source article (Krippendorff's alpha 0.773). 3 For all source articles, coders were asked to code if they contained elite or nonelite voices as the second coding category (Krippendorff's alpha 0.726 4 ). Elite voices were defined as politicians, celebrities, businessmen and so forth, whereas nonelite voices were described as citizen voices, users whose names were cited, and references to generic groups of users. For this task, the coders were given the sentence that included the search terms plus the two preceding and following sentences.

Assuming that there are no more relevant articles outside of C2, estimates for the entire corpus were calculated based on the codings. Fluctuations, for example in the size of the newspaper, were thus normalized.

sections.paper_id: Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: We analyzed the topic structure used in the source articles using unsupervised topic clustering Latent Dirichlet Allocation (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003) with the tosca package (Koppers et al. forthcoming) for R (R Core Team 2017).

As preprocessing steps, we deleted all sentences that matched one of the regular expressions (see Supplementary material), deleted numbers, punctuation, removed stop words using the snowball stop word list (Snowball Stemmer Project 2017a, 2017b), and transformed all words to lowercase. For the LDA, we used only words that appear more than five times in the corpus. In previous projects in communication studies, LDA has already been successfully applied to journalistic texts (Jacobi, van Atteveldt, and Welbers 2016;Puschmann and Scheffler 2016). Using LDA as an additional way to code the topic structure makes our study feasible. LDA reliably generates topic distributions of large text corpora based on word co-occurrences. In the language of LDA, topics are expressed in probability distributions across word lists. For instance, "billion/percent/investors/stock" could be the first four words of such a word list, where the first word is the most probable within this topic, the second one the second most probable etc. Within each topic, i.e., in each of these lists, each word in the corpus appears once in different locations. The words "billion/percent/investors/stock" thus occur in every other topic, as well, but not as prominently. Two human coders labeled the generated topics using a coding instruction with 16 predetermined topics (Krippendorff's alpha 0.759). For comparability, these topics follow previous studies on social media sourcing. This labeling ensures that two coders associate the word lists with the same pattern, in other words, that they maintain consistency of meaning. This is a common topic validation method (Maier et al. 2018, 7). For example, both coders labeled the topic "billion/percent/investors/stock" as "economy."

The LDA was calculated on C2 (30 clusters with variables alpha and eta set to 1/30), as it probably contains all relevant articles, i.e., all articles with social media sources. Based on the probability distributions, which were calculated automatically, each word in a given article must be assigned exactly to one of the topics. In order to assign topics to the individual articles, a threshold must be defined, that is, a word count beyond which an article is assigned to a topic. In this case, this threshold was set at a minimum of ten words from a certain topic. This means that articles could appear in the analysis multiple times, which is why the accumulated share of all topics exceeds 100%. After being generated by LDA in this way, the thematic subcorpora were validated: coders assigned 1,426 articles to the predefined topics (see Supplementary material for detailed report of recall and precision for the subcorpora).

Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: Ratio of source articles referencing content from Facebook or Twitter in The New York Times, The Guardian, and S€ uddeutsche Zeitung with 95% pointwise confidence intervals sourced less until 2012/2013, followed by a rapid increase. In 2016, Facebook was sourced with similar frequency in The Guardian and in The New York Times. The increase in the use of Twitter as a source after 2014 is as distinct as in The New York Times and ultimately probably slightly higher than in The New York Times. Since 2009, Twitter sourcing has increased at least by a factor of 5; in the same period, Facebook sourcing has at least tripled. In recent years, the Twitter curve has shown a kink, indicating a halt or even a decrease in Twitter sourcing.

In S€ uddeutsche Zeitung, Twitter was sourced in 1-2% of all articles since 2013 (N ¼ 3,918). Since then, this trend has flatlined. A similar number of Twitter sources were used in The New York Times in 2012 and in The Guardian in 2010. Until this point, this increase was continuously weak without any apparent plateaus. The trend in sourcing Facebook (N ¼ 2,589) is different: After a plateau around 2011 and 2012, similar to the trend in The New York Times and The Guardian, its use has been increasing considerably. However, its highest level of about 1% is still very low compared to the other newspapers.

It is striking that in 2016, S€ uddeutsche Zeitung sourced Facebook and Twitter equally. In the same year, The New York Times sourced Twitter at least 50% more often than Facebook, The Guardian even about 150% more often.

Considering only the increase in sourcing over time (see Figure 2), we see both platforms reach zero growth or even decrease in every newspaper (except S€ uddeutsche Zeitung -Twitter), followed by a new phase of rapid growth. This new growth phase lasted until the end of the period we studied (except for The Guardian -Twitter). However, it seems that growth has been slowing down, except for social media references in The New York Times.

sections.header_raw: Innovation Attributes: Topics in Source Articles
sections.header_norm: innovation attributes topics in source articles
paragraphs: In addition to the sections "politics," "foreign politics," "economy," "culture/arts," "sports," "media/tech," "science/health," and "food," the LDA generated particular topic clusters for the following topics: "conflict" (mainly war in foreign countries), "crime," "incidents" (e.g., breaking news situations such as natural disasters, terror attacks, and riots), "digital" (e.g., platforms regulation, privacy issues, whistle-blowers, and cyber security) and "movements" (such as civil right movements, NGO campaigns, populist movements, discrimination-related topics, racism, and sexism), "youth" (e.g., teenage lifestyles

Comparison of the ratio of Facebook or Twitter sources assigned to elite or non-elite sources with 95% pointwise confidence intervals and education), and "tourism" (see Figure 4). Furthermore, there is a category "other" with articles that could not be assigned to any of the categories. Although this is the largest category for all newspapers, it does not mean that a large number of articles could not be assignedthe proportion of articles that were assigned exclusively to "other" or could not be assigned to any of the topics was less than 1% for each subcorpus.

Because LDA is using a soft-clustering approach that assigns several topics to one article, it just means that many articles contain words assigned to the category "other." These topics include generic language such as frequently used phrases, which are usually a part of LDA results.

In line with the findings from the quantitative analysis, articles with Twitter sources dominate the topic categories. The topic areas with the strongest representation in The Guardian and The New York Times are "incidents," "crime," "culture/arts," "media/tech," "sports," and "politics"these are the contexts in which journalists most often work with Twitter sources. In The Guardian, the difference between Twitter and Facebook use is more pronounced than in The New York Times: For example, in The Guardian, the LDA assigned more than three times as many source-articles from Twitter than from Facebook to the topic "crime." In The New York Times, the ratio is 3:4 on the same topic.

The Guardian does not source Facebook nearly as often as Twitter, especially on the topics "politics," "economy," and "sports" (Twitter-Facebook ratio: 4:1). The New York Times has the same ratio only for the topic of "sports." The strongest Facebook topics in both newspapers are "crime" and "media/tech." The New York Times also sources Facebook heavily on "culture/arts" and "politics"; The Guardian sources it for "international politics" and "movements." To summarize, journalists frequently source Facebook and Twitter on the topics of "crime," "media/tech," "politics," and "culture/arts."

The topic structure is similar for both platforms. Particularly interesting, however, are topics that only appear in one social medium, or topics with a strong disparity between Facebook and Twitter sources. For example, The Guardian often uses Facebook sources for adolescent topics, e.g., a report on students suspended "from a Church of England comprehensive for setting up a hate group about their teacher on Facebook" (The Guardian, January 12, 2009).

In The New York Times, Facebook is often associated with a lack of cyber security; one article, for example, mentions foreign hackers who "posted Iranian jokes to their Facebook pages" (The New York Times, May 29, 2014). Twitter lives up to its reputation as an alternative "news wire" (Petrovic et al. 2013) in the S€ uddeutsche Zeitung and The New York Times and is often used to report on incidents ("Katherine Selby, who is from Nottingham, England, was vacationing with her family in Campagnano di Roma, just outside Rome, when the wardrobe doors began 'shaking like crazy', she wrote on Twitter" -The New York Times, August 23, 2016). In addition, Twitter in the S€ uddeutsche Zeitung and The Guardian appears to often be used in the context of health communication, in the latter, in particular in connection with the National Health Service.

Apart from this, S€ uddeutsche Zeitung has fewer similarities with The Guardian and The New York Times as these newspapers have among one another. The most used Twitter topics are "incidents," "culture/arts," "digital," "economy," and "international."

The topics with the most Facebook sources are "crime," "culture/arts," "digital," "international," und "politics." S€ uddeutsche Zeitung journalists also source both platforms on topics like "culture/arts," "digital," and "international." Its Twitter-Facebook ratio is more similar to The New York Times than to The Guardian. Facebook is sourced more often than Twitter on the topics "crime" and "politics."

sections.paper_id: Von Nordheim et al. - 2018 - Sourcing the Sources An analysis of the use of Twitter and Facebook as a journalistic source over 1
paragraphs: Our analysis of the quantitative increase of social media sourcing in journalistic practices (RQ1) has shown that in most cases, we can rule out that diffusion of the innovation of social media has reached the stagnation phase. Only Twitter sourcing in The Guardian has decreased slightly, which could indicate stagnation. In all other cases, diffusion is not yet complete. We could not confirm results from previous studies which indicate that increase has already stagnated.

One interesting result in this context is the fact that the increase of social media sourcing reached a plateau in almost all cases at the beginning of the decade. 6 Facebook seems to have entered this phase of stagnation (or even of decline) earlier than Twitter. As the example of the USA shows, journalists' source usage surprisingly correlates directly with the development of user numbers. In the years when user development slows down (Pew Research Center 2017), source use also flattens. This falls in line with previous research, for example by Engesser and Humprecht (2015), who describe a stage of disillusionment with Twitter in the USA as early as 2011. This was followed, however, by a new phase of rapid increase in social media sourcing, this time unrelated to user numbers.

We can only speculate about the reasons for the growth period that followed, such as changes in business strategy after the initial public offering. It was reported that Facebook changed its algorithm to present more newsworthy content in order to challenge Twitter as the news channel among social media (Somaiya 2014). Another aspect of Facebook's challenge was to push for hashtag use (Busemann 2013, 398) and the news app "FB Newswire." Facebook's objective was to "make it easier for journalists and newsrooms to find, share and embed newsworthy content from Facebook in the media they produce" (Facebook 2014). Maybe the strategy effectively made Facebook a more relevant source, i.e., a more compatible innovation, for journalists. What is certain, though is that Facebook has since become an important traffic referrer for journalism brandsespecially at the end of 2014, when Facebook-led traffic onto news sites increased massively (Social Media Index 2017). Maybe this increasing interdependence also influenced journalists' sourcing practices, perhaps because of social media editors, Facebook monitoring, or similar changes in the newsrooms.

Our study showed a changed ratio of elite/nonelite sources during the diffusion phase (RQ2). Recent findings on the elite focus of Twitter could be confirmed across all newspapers. The use of nonelite voices from Twitter has not increased considerably in any of the newspapers. The general increase in sourcing Twitter is obviously primarily due to an increase in elite sources. Apparently, Twitter has become established as a social medium for professional communicators, such as politicians and celebrities. The hypothesis of normalization can only be confirmed with regard to Twitter; however, interestingly, in Facebook source articles, elite and nonelite voices are balanced across SOURCING THE SOURCES all newspapers. We cannot assume a change in audience relationships here, either, since it seems that Facebook has been used as a source for elite and non-elite voices from the beginning, and at a similar level, but it would be wrong to jump to the conclusion that Facebook is the "citizens" social medium' compared to "elitist" Twitter: Twitter sources cite nonelite sources similarly often as Facebook. The Guardian even uses Twitter more often for non-elite sources than Facebook, confirming its reputation as "frontrunner in adopting Twitter" (Broersma and Graham 2013, 451).
Our analysis provided insight into which innovation attributes are associated with social media in the system of journalism (RQ3).

Twitter is a much more important source of coverage than Facebook across almost all topics and all newspapers reviewedalthough Facebook is the more important source of news for users in all countries (Newman et al. 2017). This suggests that Twitter, despite its much smaller community, offers compatibility advantages for journalists compared to its competitor Facebook. The difference between Twitter and Facebook is particularly large in the UK, less so in the USAin Germany, Facebook is even sourced more often than Twitter on certain topics like politics. The reason for this seems to be the political culture of Germany, where, in contrast to the Anglo-American system, political communication is not driven by individual politicians, but rather by the parties, which tend to communicate via their Facebook pages rather than via Twitter accounts. In this regard, Facebook is more compatible than Twitter as a source for political reporting in Germany.

We observe an indirect correlation between the number of platform users and source use: Twitter plays the biggest role as a source where the ratio of Twitter news usage to Facebook news usage is greatest (in ca. UK 1:2, USA 1:3, Germany 1:6, Newman et al. 2017), confirming Gulyas's finding that journalists from countries with "large potential Twitter audiences use Twitter more frequently" (2017,525). Another reason for the different social media usage might be different degrees of competitiveness in the media markets of northern European and Atlantic countries (Broersma and Graham 2013, 452). Against this backdrop, the rise of social media sourcing could also be an indication for the decline of journalistic business models that forces journalists to use social media to reduce costs of information gathering (Weaver et al. 2007).

The topics for which social media are most commonly sourced are similar across all countries: Among them are soft news topics such as crime, media, technology, arts and culture, as well as hard news topics such as domestic and international politics, breaking-news events or reporting on movements. This topic area highlights the relative advantages of the source social media for journalists.

To summarize, the rapid increase in virtually every newspaper indicates that diffusion of social media sourcing has not yet come to an end. Against this backdrop, the potential impact of corporate strategies on journalism is certainly problematic. However, further research into this second rising of social media is needed to analyze its exact reasons. Presumably, various social, technical, and economic dynamics are reinforcing each other: increased efforts to attract the attention of journalists, more and more young journalists with an affinity to social media in the newsrooms (Gillis and Johnson 2015), increased convergence of distribution channels and increased presence of elites in social media et cetera.

The simplifications of the theoretical framework become apparent at this point: we work on the assumption that the use of social media sources by print journalists constitutes the adaption of an innovation, as though it were a tool to be discovered that has the sole purpose of improving working processes. In reality, it differs in that its benefit is not a constantto put it simply: while an agricultural machine always promises gains in efficiency as a constant, the added value of social media sources is dependent on context. Its benefit changes, for example, when there is a change in the user structures on a platform. These factors, in turn, are dependent on technology-related innovations such as the spread of smartphones, faster mobile data transmission rates, and change in algorithms. In a word, the dynamics of adaption in social practice are apparently dependent on more variables than the dissemination of a technical innovation. The original model of Rogers (2003) considers the diffusion of an innovation driven by the mechanisms of an encapsulated marketwhen exogenous factors are added, the model becomes concomitantly unsuitable because it does not take account of these factors. If one decides to use the model nonetheless, misleading inferences may result. Despite these limiting conditions, we think an analysis using the innovation diffusion model is meaningful: Against the backdrop of the research on social media usage presented, we are of the opinion that the market mechanisms in the model can plausibly explain our observations at the current stage of development.

The comparative perspective has proven to be fruitful. The elite focus ascertained in previous social media research studies was proven only for Twitter. We even observe increasing path dependence: Twitter is becoming increasingly elitist, at least in the mirror of journalism. This, in turn, makes it more attractive to elites and thus even more popular as an elite source, et cetera. On Facebook, however, elite and nonelite voices seem to be heard equally. Based on these findings, it is important to warn against generalizations from often overly Twitter-focused research. A clear soft news focus, as found in many early studies on social media sources, could not be confirmed. Furthermore, the results of this study make it doubtful as to whether social media can be spoken of as a single innovation. The image of an innovation cluster (Rogers 2003, 240) seems to be more suitable here; parallels and, thus, generalizations between its representatives are possible, but they have to be examined by comparative studies. Finally, it must be emphasized that regarding the analysis of sourcing practices, we have examined only one possible way of adaption. Investigations of other levels on which adaptation takes place would provide a broader picture.

The country comparison was enlightening, as well. Journalists of S€ uddeutsche Zeitung are far less reliant on social media than their colleagues from The New York Times and The Guardian. The results might indicate that social media sourcing in Atlantic countries differs from northern European countries in terms of quantity and thematic setting. The fact that the newspapers are leading media in their countries does not mean that they are necessarily representative of journalistic culture in their countries. More representative comparative studies are needed to confirm these assumptions.

The main limitation of our study manifests itself in this quote from a Guardian correction article: "What we should have made clear is that the incident was taken from a post on a Facebook page" (The Guardian, May 18, 2014). This shows that many references to social media remain implicit and therefore hidden from content analysis. At the same time, social media are often quoted from secondary sources such as news agencies (De Dobbelaer, Paulussen and Maeseele, 2013). It would therefore be important to generate up-to-date qualitative evidence, for example from interviews or observational studies, in order to contextualize the findings gained here. Moreover, our method setting, designed for a long-term comparison, has the disadvantage that a detailed analysis was not possible. However, the results of this study provide a starting point for further content analyses, for example, on the function of social media sources in coverage, or more precise information on the cited social media actors. Also, the study should be repeated in the coming years to confirm the tendency of stagnation in the increase of social media sourcing.

In reality, it differs in that its benefit is not a constantto put it simply: while an agricultural machine always promises gains in efficiency as a constant, the added value of social media sources is dependent on context. Its benefit changes, for example, when there is a change in the user structures on a platform. These factors, in turn, are dependent on technology-related innovations such as the spread of smartphones, faster mobile data transmission rates, and change in algorithms. In a word, the dynamics of adaption in social practice are apparently dependent on more variables than the dissemination of a technical innovation. The original model of Rogers (2003) considers the diffusion of an innovation driven by the mechanisms of an encapsulated marketwhen exogenous factors are added, the model becomes concomitantly unsuitable because it does not take account of these factors. If one decides to use the model nonetheless, misleading inferences may result. Despite these limiting conditions, we think an analysis using the innovation diffusion model is meaningful: Against the backdrop of the research on social media usage presented, we are of the opinion that the market mechanisms in the model can plausibly explain our observations at the current stage of development.

The comparative perspective has proven to be fruitful. The elite focus ascertained in previous social media research studies was proven only for Twitter. We even observe increasing path dependence: Twitter is becoming increasingly elitist, at least in the mirror of journalism. This, in turn, makes it more attractive to elites and thus even more popular as an elite source, et cetera. On Facebook, however, elite and nonelite voices seem to be heard equally. Based on these findings, it is important to warn against generalizations from often overly Twitter-focused research. A clear soft news focus, as found in many early studies on social media sources, could not be confirmed. Furthermore, the results of this study make it doubtful as to whether social media can be spoken of as a single innovation. The image of an innovation cluster (Rogers 2003, 240) seems to be more suitable here; parallels and, thus, generalizations between its representatives are possible, but they have to be examined by comparative studies. Finally, it must be emphasized that regarding the analysis of sourcing practices, we have examined only one possible way of adaption. Investigations of other levels on which adaptation takes place would provide a broader picture.

The country comparison was enlightening, as well. Journalists of S€ uddeutsche Zeitung are far less reliant on social media than their colleagues from The New York Times and The Guardian. The results might indicate that social media sourcing in Atlantic countries differs from northern European countries in terms of quantity and thematic setting. The fact that the newspapers are leading media in their countries does not mean that they are necessarily representative of journalistic culture in their countries. More representative comparative studies are needed to confirm these assumptions.

The main limitation of our study manifests itself in this quote from a Guardian correction article: "What we should have made clear is that the incident was taken from a post on a Facebook page" (The Guardian, May 18, 2014). This shows that many references to social media remain implicit and therefore hidden from content analysis. At the same time, social media are often quoted from secondary sources such as news agencies (De Dobbelaer, Paulussen and Maeseele, 2013). It would therefore be important to generate up-to-date qualitative evidence, for example from interviews or observational studies, in order to contextualize the findings gained here. Moreover, our method setting, designed for a long-term comparison, has the disadvantage that a detailed analysis was not possible. However, the results of this study provide a starting point for further content analyses, for example, on the function of social media sources in coverage, or more precise information on the cited social media actors. Also, the study should be repeated in the coming years to confirm the tendency of stagnation in the increase of social media sourcing.
sections.preview_head: Our analysis of the quantitative increase of social media sourcing in journalistic practices (RQ1) has shown that in most cases, we can rule out that diffusion of the innovation of social media has reached the stagnation phase. Only Twitter sourcing in The Guardian has decreased slightly, which could indicate stagnation. In all other cases, diffusion is not yet complete.

The data basis contains the full corpora. In the case of The New York Times and The Guardian, they were obtained from the digital news archive Nexis, in the case of S€ uddeutsche Zeitung from the publisher. To prepare the corpora for analysis, they were cleaned: , duplicates with identical IDs were deleted, article versions with identical titles within a week from the Nexis data sets were reduced to the longest article, and articles in foreign languages and texts from live coverage were excluded from the analysis. Since we plan to only include newspaper articles in the study, and Nexis offers no way to remove articles from other sources (online, foreign-language editions) that occur unsystematically (for instancee.g., using XML tags), we had to cleanse our data manually. 3. We include every article in the sourced sample that refers to information available on the social media platform, such as texts, photos, videos, status updates, friend requests and new groups. Furthermore, observations that can only be made visiting the platforms are defined as social media source if they refer to a specific event. Articles, for which information was clearly gathered via another source, are excluded. 4. Inter-coder reliability was measured using the identical source -articles. In 3.8% of the cases, on average, coders were unable to allocate a label in the second category because they were uncertain whether the cited source belonged to the elite or non-elite category. 5.
